danger_signals.visual_form: Health bar reduction (replenishable via collected bugs)
danger_signals.meaning: Player character Simba taking damage from enemies or environmental hazards
Danger from enemies that must be avoided or jumped upon to defeat

danger_signals.visual_form: Water/lava lakes, bottomless pits, rolling boulders, falling stalactites
danger_signals.meaning: Instant death or health loss from environmental interaction

danger_signals.visual_form: Distinct black spider sprites (variant of collectible bugs)
danger_signals.meaning: Damaging insects to avoid, contrasting with healing bugs

danger_signals.visual_form: Replenishable roar meter (used by young Simba)
danger_signals.meaning: Loss of stunning/puzzle-solving ability when exhausted

safety_signals.visual_form: Collectible bug sprites scattered throughout levels
safety_signals.meaning: Health restoration and meter extension (rarer variants increase health and roar permanently)
Safe restart point if player loses a life without requiring level restart
Grants continue tokens, allowing restart from last stage on game over

safety_signals.visual_form: Hidden areas triggered by finding specific bugs in certain levels
safety_signals.meaning: Safe opportunity to earn extra lives and continues via Timon/Pumbaa minigames

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Lion cub and adult lion sprite
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Transforms from young cub (jumping-based combat) to adult lion (scratching, mauling, throwing attacks)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Pride Lands geography (levels based on film scenes including Elephant Graveyard, jungle canopy, etc.)

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Enemy sprite designs representing Scar and his forces
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Various hostile entities and creatures that Simba must defeat through the game progression
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Dark volcanic/corrupted environments correlating with Scar's control (visible in later stages leading to final confrontation)

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Playable in bonus stages with distinct mechanics (bug collection rather than combat)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Jungle Haven sequences representing safe refuge areas

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Young Simba phase (first half of game)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Smaller cub sprite with limited combat animations (primarily jumping)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Simba defeats enemies by jumping on them; roar used for stunning enemies and puzzle solving

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Adult Simba phase (second half of game)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Larger adult lion sprite with expanded animation set
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Access to advanced combat moves: scratching, mauling, throws; roar mechanic transitions to combat utility

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Environmental darkening and volcanic/fire-themed level aesthetics
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemy patterns intensify; Boss encounter with Scar as climax event

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Transition to Hakuna Matata and similar refuge level designs
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Shift to bonus stage minigames with different controls and objectives (bug collection vs. combat)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Health meter critically low or visual fade indicating death
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player respawns at last checkpoint or stage start depending on continue availability

environmental_storytelling.technique: Level design based on film narrative
environmental_storytelling.example: Levels adapted from Disney's 1994 film scenes including Elephant Graveyard, jungle canopy, volcanic regions; two levels (Hakuna Matata and Be Prepared) adapted from scenes cut from final film

environmental_storytelling.technique: Character sprite animation by Disney animators
environmental_storytelling.example: Sprites and backgrounds drawn by Walt Disney Feature Animation staff, embedding film-quality visual narrative into game world
Visual and mechanical shift from cub to adult Simba communicates story progression and power increase without explicit narrative text
Different enemy types appear in contextual environments (e.g., creatures matching Pride Lands fauna in appropriate zones)
Water/lava lakes, bottomless pits, rolling boulders, and stalactites placed to guide player movement and communicate traversal danger

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Jumps on enemies to defeat them; uses roar (limited resource) to stun enemies or solve puzzles
Continues movement but with vulnerability to single-hit KO from remaining hazards
Executes scratching, mauling, and throwing attacks; roar integrated into combat arsenal
Can interact with hidden bug locations to trigger bonus stages
Patrol and attack on contact; defeated by young Simba jumping or adult Simba combat moves
Time-limited bug collection with spider avoidance; 10-bug increments trigger reward escalation
Collects falling bugs until bad bug or item reaches screen bottom; mechanical simplicity contrasts main game

notes: The Lion King SNES is a narrative-driven platformer where visual communication relies heavily on Disney film adaptation fidelity. Danger is communicated through environmental hazard visibility (water, lava, pits, projectiles) and enemy sprite distinctiveness. Safety is signaled through collectible bugs (health), checkpoints, and bonus stage transitions. Political faction dynamics are minimal (good vs. evil binary) but reinforced through sprite quality (Disney-animated player character vs. enemy designs) and environmental theming. World-state progression is primarily visual (sprite size/animation change for Simba transformation) rather than explicitly textual. The game uses hierarchical visual design: player sprite priority, enemy distinctiveness, environmental hazards, and background storytelling collectively communicate game state without UI-heavy displays.
